So if you are not starving, you are not losing fat. Okay. Okay. All right. All right. Welcome to fat loss. Now that the caveat is can you mobilize fat at the rate that you are requiring energy? And that's where the kicker is. Where really if you can mobilize a half a pound of fat every day, you turn that into the magic number of 1750 calories, then anything beyond that you might pull from nitrogen stores also known as muscle mass, all that good stuff. You guys like to ask a lot of kind of advanced relative questions that may be relevant to you, but have nothing to do with any of the people you work with. Most of the people you work with have no muscle to lose. So I wouldn't really worry about that very much. Okay. So next one. Okay. What is BMI and how do you calculate it? All right. So I'm not going to go over that. You can find a calculator just fine. I just need to go over kind of what it means. And a lot of people may not find the use or kind of write it off. So by BMI stands for body mass index, it's a formula that combines your height and weight to generate a value. And that value is used with almost statistical ubiquity, like across the globe, to and use it to identify health risks associated with weight. So the biggest meat had to come back is like, well, I got more muscle mass. My BMI doesn't matter. That's totally wrong. Basically, the higher your the higher your weight is relative to your height, the more at risk you are for health conditions related to lifestyle, regardless of the muscle mass you have. And in fact, I would argue the more muscle mass you have, the more at risk you are because it is metabolically active tissue. Okay, so the more organ stress you have with the more there's a reason why big body builders drop that at 50 their BMI is pretty f and high. Okay, it is not the drugs, it's that they are massive people and it just so happens that drugs make massive people. So okay. Did anyone's like mine get chatter because I told him I was like, I'm good my BMI is I was like, dude, but I have like way more muscle. He's like, yeah, you're still at risk. And I was like, like, you think your heart doesn't work harder when you're more buff? Come on. Like, I mean, if the fat is stored in between your organs, that's a different monster that has more of a genetic component that leads to things like diabetes. And the more generically fat you are, the more fat you will have stored between your organs. Therefore, like, if you have lower fat percentage, you will have lower fat storage in your organs. So certain organs will have less stress physiologically. But the more muscle mass you have, you don't think everything is working harder to support that? It is. So just because you're buff doesn't mean you're going to live forever. Although that would be awesome if the pump were the cure. Okay. All right. So what should I do after the challenge? This is something that gets asked a lot too. Why is there no fat in this? Quite literally every living thing ever needs fat in it to be alive. So there's just no added fat. Like yes, plants have fat in them. It makes up cell membranes and signaling molecules and hormones and all sorts of cool stuff. So if an organism is alive, it does contain fat. Fat is necessary for life. It is used to build up cells and not the eight feet by six feet kind, but the kind that I like to study in school for a really long time. So fat is literally in everything. It may not be in large amounts, but that is okay. The essentiality of fat is, well, people probably got enough stored. So like if you can mobilize it for your needs, I would take advantage of that. So really the language is that there is fat in this. There is just no fat coming from butter or oil. That's it. There's fat in the meat. There's fat in the plant material. The only foods that are truly fat for you are the ones that are so heavily processed and recrystallized and purified that they call them gummy worms. Does that make sense? There is fat in here. There's just not fat coming from butter or oil. Does anybody still have panic attacks about this? And from a health standpoint, they won't be deficient of fat because they have fat and they will just use that fat. To reiterate, this is the six week challenge. You know vitamin A is a fat-soluble vitamin and people like to learn new science. You need to have fat to absorb fat-soluble stuff. You have 10 years of vitamin A stored in your liver, just so you know. And another 50 years of vitamin D stored in your fat. So you're cool for a couple of weeks without carry gold. Okay. There's a million reasons for this, guys. And I'm not going to go into all of them now, one because I don't even know what all of them are. There's the obvious of like the person is not following it or the person thinks they're following it and following it incorrectly. The person might be lying. There's such thing as the law of constrained energy expenditure which I subscribe to which is essentially like, okay, I burned 200 calories during my workout, but then I lounge for 200 calories on the couch and the gating that whole calorie burn fiasco. So there's sleep components. There's drug components. There's what is your non-exercise activity thermogenesis which kind of falls in line with am I turning into a zombie on days that I'm not training and that makes up for the calories that I burned. So basically, this is where the therapy comes in. They're not stuck for any really crazy physiological reason. Okay, so yeah, calories and calories out doesn't work, yada, yada, yada, okay. Yeah, we can control the calories going in. So like, it's your fault, I'm still fat and that's one of the reasons I don't like templates in the long term is because you are allowing people to put the responsibility of their behavior change on to you when ultimately it is their responsibility to change their behavior. So all you're doing is nailing a picture frame in with a sledgehammer, getting them to like you and then becoming a coach. Your relationship is going from transactional to transformational. Does that make sense? And the diet plan, the template is a transaction. Here is a plan you can follow, it will probably do something, will it do everything? Absolutely not. And in order to do everything, you have to transform the way you behave and this is the place to do that. Okay, does that make sense? Cool. Oh, Splenda and Aspartame bad for me, hold on, I'm thirsty. There's proportionalities and coefficients that are like, they're bigger people, it makes sense, it's a relative change thing. That's where like a 400 pound man losing 40 pounds is like a little bit, not really as crazy as a 100 pound girl losing 10. So just think about it in those terms. There's nothing you can't get in those that you need from anywhere else. Protein powder is an obvious good one. Multivitamin is cheap insurance. Vitamin D, everybody is low on. So if you can convince a person to spend an extra $200 a month on buying protein powder multivitamins, then you can give them a more personalized experience, which is going to work better anyway. Does that make sense? Until Prestige Lab comes out. Yes, right. I would trust something that I made, but like I'm... Next one? I have a question right here. What's up, Cassie? What's up, Alex? So I have actually two questions. One being, do you do our less experience with the Green Play? I was talking with Monica and we all got wowed by your six-week program and we got results like crazy. Therefore, we're like, oh my gosh, Alex, you're the man. We're going to do this. Now, if we don't deliver that same effect to our members, because the Green Play is like, hey, if you don't meet your 20 pound goal, it's okay. You're in this for life. From my experience, I've had more of a percentage fall out than when they do the challenge and we wow them, they lose like 35 pounds, 25 pounds, whatever it may be. If that trend continues, would you suggest still sticking with the Green Play for us? Yes. So everything would be 100% down to the numbers. So if I had... And it would have to track from 100 people walking in the door. So if I had 100 people walking in the door and let's say I get 80 of them to sign up on the Green Play and then... And also, I would be looking into... That sounds like a conviction thing because there's a lot of people who are here who are doing the Green Play who are not seeing that. And so I think it probably relates back to the first thing you said, which is like, I believe so much in this thing. What about this? And a lot of it comes down to like, well, I don't think it'll work as well because they're not betting on themselves. Like, it depends on which way you want to sell yourself. You know what I mean? If I was trying to sell myself on the challenge, I would sell myself on the challenge the way that we always do with the bet. If I was trying to sell myself on the Green Play, then I would say, listen, we need to create this behavior change in you that is not dependent on an external money factor for you to have a long-term success. So like, I could sell myself either way on it and I would suggest you sell yourself either way on it. But the reason that the Green Play is preferable in my mind is that more people will sign up on the front end and to the vast majority of the people in the room, the lifetime value of those customers is significantly higher. That's why. But the actual... If you think about it, the actual template and the deliverable for either of them should be identical and the only thing that has changed is the betting component. And so it's just, can we deliver a level of accountability that they will follow through with? Yeah, I love both plays. I'm just... My two cents. And then my second question is real quick. What is a good HDP attrition percentage and would you suggest to do anything different in HDP as opposed to what we do for the members as far as value stacking? The attrition on HDP is typically really, really, really, really low. And if you're having a higher attrition rate, then I would talk to like the guys who've done really well with it, talk to Ryan Karris, talk to Dave Regula, talk to Joe Pasalacqua, all those guys who really, really well with HDP and just figure out what the whole is. I can't remember the second part of your question. Anything different for HDP as far as value stacking the program as opposed to the members? The majority of the value with HDP is that it's one on four. You know what I mean? Like at the end of the day, if you're giving someone meal plan grocery list for preparation instructions, eating out guy, blah, blah, blah, blah, and then you're just using it off the template that you now actually are manually making instead of having the truly templated version, but it's being personalized to that person. Those are the two main components, is that they're a trainer-to-client ratio in the gym and the actual personalization and the adjustments of the plan on an ongoing basis, rather than just for this period of time. What are the names you just mentioned that were good at HDP? Ryan Carrier is Joe Pasalacqua. Who else did I name? Dave Regula. They all work with me. Next one. What are your thoughts on intermittent and fasting? I get eight hours every night. Does it play well in this six-week challenge? Can you repeat the question? I'm sorry. Does intermittent fasting work well with the six-week challenge? Okay. So to give you more serious answer with intermittent fasting, I think there is some therapeutic value in populations that you probably shouldn't be working with, for one. And two, intermittent fasting is pretty effectively employed in terms of logistical situations. Like I use it a lot with people who are shift workers, like firefighters, police officers, nurses, people that have whack-ass schedules that can only eat within a certain window. So they are intermittently fasting because they have to, and that is less stressful for them than trying to pull over the cop car to shove in meat and rice. So I think if a person's lifestyle is severely limited by what they do for work, then making the meal schedule for them in terms of when they eat, making that easier on them day to day, is going to be effective because they will adhere to it. If you're trying to force somebody to eat at a time that is not realistic to them, they will feel incompetent and they will also be irritated with you and they will fall off. So if a person only has three hours a day to eat, then eat in those three hours, and that is how it is most effectively used. Does that make sense? Yes. So if people were trying to implement that into the six-week challenge, it's fine to let them. So I think it's a matter of why is the person wanting to do it? Is there some like magical unicorn effect they're trying to get out of like eating 1500 calories now versus in three meals? They read their fat loss. They're struggling with their fat loss and they read intermittent fasting helps, so they're trying to up their fat loss. Okay, so I would not try to convince them not to do it, but when you go from the transactional relationship to the transformational one, you can say that you do not have to do this because if they feel like it's inconvenient, but they're doing it because they think they're getting better results, those results will eventually not be as fast, and so the convenience factor will be taken into account, and then you can still maintain progress by having foods you enjoy at times you would like to consume them. If they are purposefully limiting their hours of eating to get an enhanced effect, that effect, if it does exist, will eventually go away, in which case what do you do then? You just go back to a normal frickin' human eating pattern. So I think don't convince them to stop doing it because what that'll do is make them cranky with you because if for whatever reason they subconsciously decide to not follow the program perfectly the way you've laid out, then they're not going to be losing the weight because they are not intermittent fasting and you told them to stop. So that's really more of like a funky behavioral component that should be addressed after they become a lifer. Does that make sense? Yes. Okay. Thank you. Awesome, Alex. Do you want to, we had some stuff that you wanted to cover, correct? Oh, we, oh we're on time? We're out of time. Oh, okay, cool. Thank you guys. You guys did.
